text: So another interesting case that curses on a tiny dot in the right upper lobe. And there's another dot in the thymic area. This lady had endometrial cancer, operated robotically, but she wants to know if these are metastases. So do a thymectomy approach. This is my three ports.

The surgeon positions the camera at the lowest port and uses two artificial arms directly through the skin without ports, achieving wrist movements at right angles. The video shows a close-up view of the phrenic nerve, thymic area, and superior vena cava (SVC). The first port is inserted in the third intercostal space after administering local anesthetic. The surgeon uses a wristed instrument in the left hand to point out the phrenic nerve and thymus, with the reamer vein and right upper lobe visible. A blind wedge resection of the nodule in the right upper lobe is performed.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments to palpate and identify a nodular area in the right upper lobe, corresponding to scan findings, for a blind wedge resection. The procedure begins by dissecting the thymus from the pericardium and fat, moving along the right phrenic nerve to elevate the sternum, facilitating access to the thymic area.

The surgeon uses wristed bipolar instruments to precisely angle around the reamer vein and artery, visible at the top of the screen. The instruments allow safe manipulation near the phrenic nerve, removing fat from the superior vena cava (SVC) and exposing the innominate vein. The surgeon then clears fat between the innominate and reamer veins, proceeding medially along the right internal mammary artery, breaking up the pleura down to the diaphragm.

The surgeon uses a wristed instrument set at seventy watts to strip the thymus from the pericardium, moving north over the sternum and aorta. The instrument's right angle facilitates easy manipulation, and the absence of thymic hyperplasia or inflammatory reaction simplifies the procedure.

The surgeon navigates between the innominate vein and the aorta, removing fat between the reamer vein and the innominate vein to expose the right superior horn of the thymus. A large vein from the back of the sternum is encountered, prompting a switch to the Hook Ligasure instrument to divide vessels up to seven millimeters. The surgeon identifies a dark nodule within the thymus, which appears rounded and soft, and continues to strip the thymus from the aorta while examining the innominate vein for potential abnormalities.

The surgeon searches for the thymic vein draining into the innominate vein but does not find it, suggesting drainage through the sternum to the lima or rima vein. The surgeon dissects the left superior horn of the thymus using a ligature with a hook, carefully separating it from the left pleura while avoiding the phrenic nerve. The left superior horn is pulled from the neck and ligatured, leaving a small stump. The thymus is removed along with the nodule, ensuring a good margin.

The surgeon removes fat between the innominate vein and the aorta, completing the thymectomy. A subxiphoid port is used for the removal of the thymus, minimizing pain by avoiding intercostal access. The final view shows the innominate vein and neck area, with a subxiphoid drain placed. A blind wedge resection is performed, with staples used to secure the tissue, and the wedge is removed using a bag. A 20 French drain is placed at the end of the procedure.
